[Page 1]

Article

Nucleosome Stability Distinguishes Two Different Promoter Types at All Protein-Coding Genes in Yeast Graphical Abstract

Authors Slawomir Kubik, Maria Jessica Bruzzone, Philippe Jacquet, Jean-Luc Falcone, Jacques Rougemont, David Shore

Correspondence David.Shore@unige.ch

In Brief Kubik et al. demonstrate that the differential distribution of general regulatory factor (GRF) binding sites and two short DNA motifs leads to establishment of either of two nucleosome architectures at all yeast promoters. GRF binding and chromatin remodeling contributes to destabilization of the 1 nucleosome at highly transcribed genes.

Highlights

Yeast promoters form two groups based on the stability of their 1 nucleosome

Distribution of two short DNA motifs is critical for promoter chromatin architecture

Fragile nucleosomes are formed through the action of GRFs and chromatin remodelers

Kubik et al., 2015, Molecular Cell 60, 422–434 November 5, 2015 a2015 Elsevier Inc. http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.molcel.2015.10.002

Accession Numbers GSE73337

[Page 2]

Molecular Cell

Article Nucleosome Stability Distinguishes Two Different Promoter Types at All Protein-Coding Genes in Yeast Slawomir Kubik,1 Maria Jessica Bruzzone,1 Philippe Jacquet,2 Jean-Luc Falcone,3 Jacques Rougemont,2 and David Shore1,* 1Department of Molecular Biology and Institute of Genetics and Genomics in Geneva (iGE3), 30 quai Ernest-Ansermet, 1211 Geneva, Switzerland 2Swiss Institute of Bioinformatics (SIB) and Bioinformatics and Biostatistics Core Facility, School of Life Sciences, Ecole Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne, 1015 Lausanne, Switzerland 3Center for Advanced Modeling Sciences, Computer Science Department, University of Geneva, 7 route de Drize, 1227 Carouge, Switzerland *Correspondence: David.Shore@unige.ch http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.molcel.2015.10.002

SUMMARY

Previous studies indicate that eukaryotic promoters display a stereotypical chromatin landscape characterized by a well-positioned +1 nucleosome near the transcription start site and an upstream 1 nucleosome that together demarcate a nucleosome-free (or -depleted) region. Here we present evidence that there are two distinct types of promoters distinguished by the resistance of the 1 nucleosome to micrococcal nuclease digestion. These different architectures are characterized by two sequence motifs that are broadly deployed at one set of promoters where a nuclease-sensitive (‘‘fragile’’) nucleosome forms, but concentrated in a narrower, nucleosome-free region at all other promoters. The RSC nucleosome remodeler acts through the motifs to establish stable +1 and 1 nucleosome positions, while binding of a small set of general regulatory (pioneer) factors at fragile nucleosome promoters plays a key role in their destabilization. We propose that the fragile nucleosome promoter architecture is adapted for regulation of highly expressed, growthrelated genes.

INTRODUCTION Nucleosomes have been recognized for several decades now as general impediments to transcription initiation, and their precise location and occupancy at promoters is the subject of considerable interest (Lieleg et al., 2015; Struhl and Segal, 2013). Genome-wide analyses of nucleosome landscapes have revealed a general feature of eukaryotic promoters in which two well-positioned nucleosomes (designated ‘‘+1’’ and ‘‘1’’), separated by a nucleosome-free or nucleosome-depleted region (hereafter referred to as NFR) of variable length, demarcate the site of transcription initiation (Jiang and Pugh, 2009). In yeast, the transcriptional start site (TSS) at most genes is located at

approximately 12 to 13 nucleotides inside the 50 border of the +1 nucleosome, thus immediately downstream of the nucleosome-free region (NFR). The extent to which this canonical promoter nucleosome architecture is determined by the nucleosome DNA-binding specificity, transcription factors (TFs), and/ or nucleosome remodeling enzymes is controversial (Kaplan et al., 2009; Zhang et al., 2009), and its functional significance is still largely unknown. In budding yeast, where global nucleosome occupancy and position have been measured by analysis of MNase-digested chromatin for over 10 years now, the general picture that has emerged (reviewed in Lieleg et al., 2015; Rando and Winston, 2012) is that many growth-related genes, such as those encoding ribosomal proteins, ribosome biogenesis factors, or glycolytic enzymes, are characterized by particularly wide and strongly depleted NFRs (Rando and Winston, 2012; Tirosh and Barkai, 2008; Zaugg and Luscombe, 2012). In contrast, many stress-induced genes, whose promoters typically contain a TATA element, often have a narrower NFR and less strongly positioned promoter nucleosomes, which occlude TF binding sites. However, recent improvements in DNA sequencing, as well as an appreciation of the kinetic nature of the MNase digestion assay typically used to map nucleosomes, have begun to challenge this simple view. For example, several recent studies indicate that many previously annotated NFRs in budding yeast appear to be populated by MNase-sensitive nucleosome-like particles (Henikoff et al., 2011; Kent et al., 2011; Weiner et al., 2010; Xi et al., 2011) referred to as ‘‘fragile nucleosomes’’ (FNs). Evidence for unstable particles in NFRs has also been uncovered in fission yeast (Moyle-Heyrman et al., 2013), plant (Vera et al., 2014), Drosophila (Henikoff et al., 2009), and human cells (Jin et al., 2009; Ishii et al., 2015). Thus, a precise picture of promoter nucleosome configurations, and in particular the precise nature of the NFR, remains elusive. We report here a genome-wide characterization of in vivo promoter nucleosome landscapes in yeast derived from an MNase titration analysis. Our data indicate that nucleosome-free promoter regions >150 bp in length are rare or non-existent, and point instead to the presence of a distinct class of promoter nucleosome architecture in which the 1 nucleosome is in an altered, MNase-sensitive (fragile) conformation. FNs are enriched in two broadly distributed DNA sequence motifs that,

[Page 3]

Figure 1. Chromatin MNase Sensitivity Profiles Define 2 Distinct Promoter Classes

(A) Scatterplot representing dependency between MNase sensitivity and WW dinucleotide count within the central 100 bp of all genomic nucleosomes (left) or 1 nucleosomes (right). Color scale represents density of nucleosome particles; dashed line indicates the threshold used to determine the subset of FNs. (B) Map showing nucleosome occupancy of genes with well-determined TSS (van Bakel et al., 2013), (top panel) genes were aligned to the center of their 1 nucleosome, in the least (left) and most (right) digested samples, genes were clustered into 2 classes depending on the stability of their 1 nucleosome and then sorted by the occupancy of this nucleosome; (bottom panel) regions were aligned to the center of +1 nucleosome and sorted by the distance to the center of the first non-FN upstream from +1. (C) Plots representing distance from the center of the +1 nucleosome to the first non-fragile upstream nucleosome for all genes categorized based on the fragility of their 1 nucleosome. Box, 25th–75th percentile; whiskers 10th–90th percentile; dots, 5th and 95th percentile. (D) V-plots (Henikoff et al., 2011) showing size distribution of sequenced DNA fragments obtained by digestion at low MNase concentration (y axis) as a function of distance from the center of either stable 1 nucleosomes (top panel), fragile 1 nucleosomes (center panel), or NFRs (bottom panel) (x axis). See also Figure S1 for further details.

remarkably, are found in a narrower region centered on the NFRs of promoters with a 1 stable nucleosome (SN). We present evidence that the motifs recruit and/or activate the RSC chromatin-remodeling complex, which in turn acts to push away the flanking nucleosomes, generating either an NFR or the space where a FN forms. Finally, we show that strong binding of a set of general regulatory factors (GRFs) is highly specific to FN-containing promoters and provide evidence that these factors play a key role in establishing nucleosome positions at FN-containing promoters and in destabilizing FNs. RESULTS Nucleosome Stability Defines Two Distinct Promoter Architectures In the course of studying nucleosome configurations at ribosomal protein gene (RPG) promoters in yeast (Knight et al.,

2014), we noted that the estimated occupancy of certain nucleosomes was extremely sensitive to the extent of MNase digestion. To obtain a quantitative measure of this phenomenon genome-wide, we analyzed chromatin digests at six different MNase concentrations (Figure S1A) by paired-end sequencing, using a protocol that avoids DNA size selection (Henikoff et al., 2011). Analysis of these data (Figure S1B) revealed the presence of MNase-sensitive particles immediately upstream of the TSS-associated +1 nucleosome at approximately 2,000 promoters. To control for MNase cleavage bias, we calculated an occupancy ratio between the least- and most-digested samples for each mapped particle and plotted this ratio versus the frequency of preferential cut sites for MNase (WW di-nucleotides; W = A or T) within each particle (Figure 1A). This analysis does indeed show a consistent, weak trend toward increased apparent nucleosome occupancy ratio as the WW dinucleotide frequency increases from 20 to 60 per nucleosome, with most particles clustering in a relatively tight distribution around an occupancy ratio of 1. Strikingly, a group of particles (which we designate as FNs) displays a much higher occupancy ratio over the same distribution of WW dinucleotide frequencies. We conclude that the MNase sensitivity of these nucleosomes is due to an altered physical property of the particles

[Page 4]

rather than a cleavage preference of MNase for their underlying DNA sequence. We note that the FNs form a distinct group by this measure, rather than constituting the tail end of a continuum of MNase sensitivities. Furthermore, the genome-wide distribution of FNs is markedly non-random (Figure S1C), with more than 70% found at promoters and another 25% in other intergenic or un-translated regions, consistent with a previous report (Xi et al., 2011). Only 2% of FNs are found in verified open reading frames. The nucleosome occupancy patterns determined at low and high MNase concentrations for the promoter regions of all 5,019 yeast genes with a well-defined TSS (van Bakel et al., 2013) are displayed in Figure 1B. Genes were first grouped into two classes (1 FN and 1 SN; top panels) based upon occupancy ratio, as a function of WW content, of the first particle upstream of the TSS-associated +1 (see Experimental Procedures for details). The two groups were then sorted by occupancy of the 1 nucleosome measured at low MNase levels and aligned to this nucleosome. This representation reveals the notable difference between 1 FNs and the relatively MNase insensitive 1 SNs and shows that nearly 40% of all RNA polymerase II (RNAPII)-transcribed genes contain a FN at the 1 nucleosome position. In contrast, sorting according to decreasing distance between the +1 nucleosome and the first upstream SN, then aligning to the +1 nucleosome (Figure 1B, bottom panels), emphasizes a gradient in the size of the nucleosome-depleted region immediately upstream of the +1 nucleosome, and the appearance of one (or two) MNase-sensitive signals (FNs) only when the size of this region exceeds 150 bp (Figure 1C; size of NFR defined as the calculated center-to-center distance of flanking nucleosomes minus 147 bp). When including MNase-sensitive particles, NFRs >150 bp are very rarely found in promoters, or elsewhere in the genome. Conversely, we never find evidence for large (>80 bp) MNasesensitive particles in regions where the distance between two stable promoter nucleosomes is <300 bp (i.e., where the NFR length is <150 bp). Because we sequenced both ends of each MNase digestion product, we were able to determine that the minimal protected size of the particles that we designate as FNs is 100 bp of DNA, as opposed to the 140 bp lower limit observed at 1 SNs (Figure 1D). Despite this difference, several lines of evidence indicate that FNs are indeed nucleosomal particles. First, these particles are only detected when the distance between stable +1 and 1 nucleosomes leaves an intervening length of free DNA (150 bp) sufficient for formation of a canonical nucleosome. Second, genome-wide chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-seq) experiments demonstrate peaks of histone H3 and H2A enrichment at MNase-mapped FN sites (Figure S1D). Third, analysis of DNase-seq data (Zelin et al., 2012) reveals peaks of DNase sensitivity surrounding FNs, similar to those at SNs, with an average spacing difference (120 versus 160 bp) consistent with that observed in MNase maps (Figure S1E). Moreover, both the WW and SS (S = G or C) dinucleotide patterns are similar between 1 SNs and FNs and markedly different than those observed in regions we designate as genuine NFRs (Figure S1F). Fourth, FNs display MNase fragment size distributions similar to those of SNs, and clearly different than NFRs

(Figure 1D). Finally, FNs map upstream of sites of cross-linking of pre-initiation complex (PIC) components (Rhee and Pugh, 2012; Venters et al., 2011) (Figure S1G) and do not resemble signals for Mediator subunits (Jeronimo and Robert, 2014; Paul et al., 2015), arguing against the possibility that they are due to either of these large transcription complexes. At present, we do not know the physical properties of FNs but speculate that their MNase sensitivity results from an increased unwrapping of the DNA at the entry and exit points of the particles (Li et al., 2005; Polach and Widom, 1995), higher translational motion of the DNA relative to the histone complex, a deficiency in histone content, or a combination of these effects. When transcription of the 1 FN and 1 SN gene classes is compared, a significant difference is noted, with 1 FN genes displaying higher levels of RNAPII, RNA-seq, and nascent transcript (NET-seq) signals (Mann-Whitney test: p < 0.001) (Figure S2A). In addition, the 1 FN group is enriched in growthrelated genes (GO-term analysis), in contrast to the 1 SN class, which is enriched for genes related to stress response and protein catabolic functions (Figure S2B; Table S1). We therefore wondered whether the appearance of FNs is a consequence of high transcription levels. To test this idea, we used the ‘‘anchor-away’’ method (Haruki et al., 2008) to rapidly deplete RNAPII from the nucleus, which was confirmed by ChIP analysis (Figure S2C). Chromatin from RNAPII-depleted and control cells was then subjected to low- and high-dose MNase-seq analysis. The derived nucleosome maps were highly similar and showed no increase in FN occupancy or stability in the absence of RNAPII (Figure S2D). We confirmed this genome-wide conclusion by examination of an individual FNcontaining gene (CHA1), whose transcription is regulated by serine (Figures S2E and S2F). Taken together, these data indicate that FNs are not the consequence of high transcription levels and suggest instead that they might be causally linked to transcription. Frequency and Distribution of Two Sequence Motifs Molds Promoter Nucleosome Landscapes We next considered the possibility that some feature of the underlying DNA sequence wrapped around FNs generates their MNase-sensitive state. To test this idea, we began by carrying out a series of fragment swap experiments. In the first of these, we chose the FRS1/REX2 gene pair, which are divergently transcribed and share a 1 SN (Figure 2A, top panel). We replaced the DNA sequence at this nucleosome with two different 1 FN sequences of precisely the same length, from the CHA1 and RPL22B promoters, leaving all other flanking sequences unaltered. Notably, in both cases replacement with the FN sequence caused the appearance of a FN at FRS1/REX2, at approximately the same position as that of the endogenous SN (Figure 2A, middle panels). Furthermore, the generation of this 1 FN was accompanied by an increase in the levels of both FRS1 and REX2 mRNAs (Figure 2A, bottom panel) and in RNAPII bound at these genes (Figure S2G). We next performed a converse fragment swap experiment, replacing two different FN sequences at the RPS11A and RPL22B genes (Figure 2B, top panels) with the SN sequence taken from the FRS1/REX2 1 nucleosome. Once again, the character of

[Page 5]

Figure 2. Nucleosome Fragility Is Associated with Underlying DNA Sequence Features

(A) Nucleosome occupancy at the REX2/FRS1 divergent promoter region taken from genomewide MNase-seq maps at low and high MNase concentration (top panel). qPCR analysis of chromatin derived from either a wild-type strain or strains where the DNA sequence of the stable 1 nucleosome at REX2/FRS1 was replaced with the sequence of 1 FNs from the CHA1 (green) or RPL22B (orange) promoters. Chromatin was digested with 0.2 U (middle, upper panel) or 2 U (middle, lower panel) of MNase; (bottom). FRS1 and REX2 mRNA level measurements for the indicated constructs. Signals were normalized to ACT1 mRNA in each sample and then to levels in the wild-type promoter strain. The error bars in mRNA level plots represent the SD of at least three independent measurements. (B) Nucleosome occupancy for RPS11A (left) or RPL22B (right) promoters and mRNA levels measurements, as in (A), in a wild-type strain or strains where the DNA sequence of the fragile 1 nucleosome of each promoter was substituted with the sequence of the REX2/FRS1 stable 1 nucleosome. See also Figure S2 and Table S1 for further details.

the underlying DNA sequence was dominant, with the FRS1/ REX2 sequence leading to the presence of a well-positioned SN at the site of the endogenous FN at both recipient promoters (Figure 2B, middle panels). Furthermore, in both cases the presence of a stabilized nucleosome was correlated with a decrease in transcription of the linked RPG (Figure 2B, bottom panels; Figure S2H). The particularly strong effect observed at RPL22B probably results from the fact that the fragment swap also abolished Rap1 binding at this promoter, which was not the case for the RPS11A swap (Figure S2I). The results described above encouraged us to search for specific DNA sequence features at FNs that might distinguish them from SNs. To do this, we used a motif-discrimination algorithm to compare the sequences at 1 FNs to those at 1 SNs genomewide (see Experimental Procedures for details). This approach identified two short sequences that are notably enriched under FNs compared to 1 SNs and all +1 nucleosomes: (i) a poly(dA:dT) motif, hereafter referred to as polyA, and (ii) a shorter G/C-rich motif (see Figure S3A for a complete list of enriched motifs). Although the length of the identified polyA motifs varied between 5 and 8 nt, we concentrated on 7 bp-long polyA stretches that have been shown previously to be significantly overrepresented in yeast promoters in general (Dechering et al., 1998). Remarkably, both motifs also appear frequently at

non-FN gene promoters, but in these cases, they are more spatially restricted and localize to the relatively small NFR between the +1 and 1 nucleosome at these promoters (Figure 3A, 3B, S3B, and S3D). Therefore, the increasing frequency and span of both motifs closely parallels that of the stable +1 to 1 nucleosome distance genome-wide (Figures 3A and 3B), strongly implicating both motifs in defining this parameter (see Discussion). To gain insight into mechanisms that might be connected to the identified motifs, we compared their in vitro and in vivo nucleosome occupancy both at sites where we observe FNs and at all other sites (Figure S3C). PolyA tracts are relatively depleted of nucleosomes in vitro, to the same extent at NFRs, FNs, and all other nucleosomes, suggesting that they intrinsically disfavor nucleosome formation (Iyer and Struhl, 1995; Yuan et al., 2005). However, only the FN- and NFR-associated polyA tracts are more strongly nucleosome depleted in vivo (measured at high MNase) than in vitro, suggesting that additional elements specific to these sites act to deplete them of nucleosomes. G/C-rich motifs, in contrast, are relatively well occupied in vitro. Strikingly, though, the FN- and NFR-associated G/Crich motifs display a much lower relative occupancy in vivo than do the same motifs found at other sites. Taken together, these observations suggest that both motifs are linked to an active in vivo mechanism of nucleosome positioning and/or expulsion that operates at both NFRs and FNs. These motifs alone are not sufficient to recruit or trigger this activity, since we do not observe similar effects at non-promoter regions.

[Page 6]

Figure 3. Specific Sequence Motifs Shape Promoter Architecture through the Action of the RSC Chromatin Remodeler

(A) Motifs overrepresented in 1 FNs and their distribution in promoters, superimposed on nucleosome positions determined at high MNase concentration (gray) and aligned to the +1 nucleosome (as shown in Figure 1B, bottom right). (B) Motif frequency, for the two indicated sequences, as a function of distance from the midpoints of 1 FNs, NFRs, and 1 SNs smoothed by a running average with a 5-bp-wide window. (C) Maps of promoters containing 1 SNs, whose NFR width decreased by >10 bp and to a +1 to 1 distance of <190 bp following Sth1 depletion (two panels top left), or where little of no NFR shrinkage was observed (two panels bottom left). Maps are aligned to the centers of NFRs. Panels on the right show the distribution of polyA and G/C-rich motifs, as indicated, for the corresponding genes to the left. (D) Same as in (C) but aligned to centers of 1 FNs whose occupancy significantly changed (top) or did not change (bottom). See also Figure S3 for further details.

DNA Sequence Motifs Act through RSC to Position Promoter Nucleosomes Interestingly, the G/C-rich motif we identify resembles binding sites determined in vitro for both the Rsc3 and Rsc30 subunits of RSC (Badis et al., 2008). Furthermore, polyA tracts have recently been shown to activate RSC in vitro (Lorch et al., 2014). We thus decided to test for a role of RSC in controlling promoter nucleosome position and stability, again using the anchor-away method, in this case to rapidly deplete the RSC cat-

alytic subunit, Sth1, from the nucleus. We then mapped nucleosomes genomewide by MNase-seq, using an intermediate MNase concentration that would allow us to capture even subtle changes at FNs. We found that nuclear depletion of Sth1 had widespread effects at NFRs, consistent with previous studies (Figure 3C) (Badis et al., 2008; Ganguli et al., 2014; Hartley and Madhani, 2009). The same was true at regions where we identified FNs (Figure 3D). At both NFRs and FNs of many genes we observed a shift of the stable +1 and 1 nucleosomes toward each other. This shrinkage of the stable +1 to 1 nucleosome distance very likely reflects the direct action of RSC, since the regions where we observed the highest difference in nucleosome occupancy correspond to regions of Sth1 binding, as determined recently by native ChIP-seq (Ramachandran et al., 2014) (Figure S3E). Consistent with this claim, the Spearman correlation coefficient between absolute nucleosome occupancy change and Sth1 occupancy was 0.41 in 100-bp windows genome-wide (compared to 0.14 for a rapamycin-treated control strain in which Sth1 was not fused to the anchor-away tag). We identified 761 examples where Sth1 depletion resulted in a significant shrinkage (>10 bp) and collapse of the NFR (as defined by a +1/1 center-to-center distance of <190 bp), and 338 cases where there was no detectable effect (Figure 3C, left panels; Figure S3F).

[Page 7]

Figure 4. PolyA and G/C-Rich Motifs Link Promoter DNA Sequence to Nucleosome Positioning and Transcription (A) Nucleosome occupancy, measured by MNaseqPCR at either low (top) or high (bottom) MNase concentration, across the GFD2 promoter in wildtype and strains where the polyA tract was progressively shortened by point mutant substitutions (mut1 to mut3). (B and C) Nucleosome occupancy measurements, as in (A), across the SSH1 promoter of wild-type and the indicated point mutants. (D and E) Nucleosome occupancy measurements, as in (A), for strains where a C/G-rich motif was inserted in the promoters of NDE2 and FRS1, respectively. See also Figure S4 for further details.

Remarkably, polyA and G/C-rich motifs were significantly enriched within the central 100 bp of affected versus unaffected regions (Mann-Whitney test p < 0.001; Figure 3C, right panels). This result strongly suggests that RSC acts through the two motifs to ‘‘push’’ against the flanking +1 and 1 nucleosomes at NFRs, maintaining their dyad-to-dyad distance at a median value of just over 240 bp, corresponding to an NFR of 90– 95 bp. In the absence of RSC, this distance relaxes to approximately 180 bp, and thus a collapse of the NFR to 30–35 bp (Figure S3F). At affected NFRs the G/C-rich motif did not overlap with Reb1 motifs, arguing against the possibility that Reb1 mediated the observed effects (Figure S3G). We also identified a large number (502) of FN regions with high polyA and G/C-rich motif density where Sth1 depletion led to significant shrinkage of the flanking stable +1 to stable 1 nucleosome distance (Figure 3D, top panels). This in turn was associated either with decreased FN occupancy (when the +1/1 center-to-center distance was still over 300 bp) or to disappearance of the FN (when shrinkage of the ‘‘free’’ DNA between the stable +1/1 nucleosomes apparently left insufficient DNA for FN formation). Nevertheless, there were many FN-containing promoters identified (561) where Sth1 depletion had no measurable effect on flanking +1/1 nucleosome position, and the FNs remained at similar occupancy levels (Figure 3D, bottom panels). These promoters were not obviously depleted of motifs, but often contained two FNs and bound the GRF Rap1 (data not shown). We found only a small number of FNs that became stabilized or significantly increased in occupancy upon Sth1 depletion (11 in total; Figure S3I), consistent with a previous report that focused on a RSC-nucleosome interaction at the GAL1/10 promoter (Floer et al., 2010). Indeed, the nucleosome described in this study is classified as fragile in our dataset and is stabilized upon Sth1 depletion. Although the results described above implicate the polyA and G/C-rich motifs in RSC function, we decided to test more directly their involvement in determining promoter nucleosome architecture. We began by generating a series of mutants in which the polyA tracts present at the GFD2/SRO9 (shared 1 FN) and SSH1 (single 1 FN) promoters were pro-

gressively shortened (Figures 4A and 4B). At GFD2/SRO9, this led to a decrease of the distance between the surrounding +1 nucleosomes, due to an upstream shift of the SRO9 +1 nucleosome, and a concomitant decrease in the occupancy of the shared 1 FN, which remained highly MNase sensitive (Figure 4A). In contrast, shortening of the polyA tract at the SSH1 FN did not have a measurable effect on the local nucleosome pattern (Figure 4B). However, unlike the SRO9/GFD2 promoter, SSH1 also possesses a nearby G/C-rich motif. When we mutated this motif we observed a clear upstream shift of the +1 nucleosome, regardless of whether the polyA tract was present or not (Figure 4C). Again, the FN remained highly MNase sensitive. At both of the above loci, the nucleosome shifts provoked by motif mutations were associated with a decrease in transcription (Figures S4A–S4C), in the absence of any detectable effect on GRF binding (Figures S4F and S4G). To gain further insight into the significance of the G/C-rich motif, we inserted it by point mutagenesis at the NDE2 and FRS1/REX2 promoters, both of which contain 1 SNs. In both cases we observed a shift of the most nearby nucleosome away from the motif (Figures 4D and 4E). The effect was stronger for the NDE2 promoter, where we also observed an 8-fold increase in NDE2 transcription in the mutated strain (Figure S4D). This may have been a direct result of the widening of the NFR, or the movement of the +1 nucleosome to a position more favorable for PIC formation. In contrast, movement of the shared 1 SN at the FRS1/REX2 promoter led to a decrease in transcription of both genes (Figure S4E). Interestingly, we did not observe the generation of a FN at either mutant promoter, perhaps because the increased size of the NFRs provoked by G/C-rich motif insertion was still not sufficient to accommodate a nucleosome. Taken together, these data show that both the polyA and G/Crich motifs can act to repel nearby nucleosomes, and thus strongly implicate these motifs in the fine-tuning of promoter nucleosome location. However, and as was the case in general for Sth1 depletion, these data do not support the idea that the motifs are directly involved in generating the MNase-sensitive state of FNs.
[Page 8]

GRFs Play a Key Role in FN Formation and Destabilization In addition to chromatin remodelers, a class of abundant and essential TFs (GRFs, including Rap1, Abf1, Reb1, Tbf1, and Cbf1) has been implicated in promoter nucleosome exclusion (Badis et al., 2008; Ganapathi et al., 2011; Hartley and Madhani, 2009; Kent et al., 1994; Tsankov et al., 2011; Tsankov et al., 2010; Yarragudi et al., 2004), members of which are thus prime candidates for trans-acting factors involved in FN destabilization. As an initial test of this idea, we plotted the binding of five different GRFs as a function of distance from the dyad axis of either 1 FNs or 1 SNs (Figure 5A). All of the proteins examined displayed a very clear bias toward FN-containing promoters, with the average peak of binding for Abf1, Reb1, Tbf1, and Cbf1 coinciding with the predicted dyad axis of the associated FN. Interestingly, the peak of Rap1 binding averaged over all sites was instead nearly 100 bp upstream of the 1 FN dyad axis. To determine if GRF binding can induce nucleosome fragility, we first decided to insert Rap1 binding sites in the promoter of SPO20, a meiosis-specific gene that is not transcribed during growth in rich medium. We generated a strain in which a 54bp-long fragment with two closely spaced Rap1 binding sites from the RPS11A promoter was inserted 190 bp upstream from the ATG of SPO20 and immediately upstream of both polyA and G/C-rich motifs present in this promoter (Figure S5A; left middle). For an unknown reason, these two motifs alone do not generate a substantial NFR at the SPO20 promoter, though they are present in the small linker between two SNs (Figure S5A, top left panel). Insertion of Rap1 binding sites upstream of these motifs, however, led to significant chromatin rearrangements (Figure S5A, bottom left panel), including the formation of a novel, low-occupancy FN very close to the inserted Rap1 binding sites and a major re-positioning of flanking SNs. These changes were associated with strong Rap1 binding, as expected (Figure S5A top right panel), and a dramatic increase in RNAPII promoter binding (Figure S5A; bottom right panel). The chromatin remodeling associated with Rap1 binding at SPO20 suggests that Rap1 (and perhaps other GRFs) can act as major drivers of FN formation, both by widening the gap between stable +1 and 1 nucleosomes and by destabilizing the nucleosome that subsequently forms there. To explore directly the role of individual GRFs in the formation of FNs genome wide, we employed the anchor-away method to rapidly deplete these essential proteins from the nucleus. We first examined Rap1, which we showed recently binds to most of the 138 RPG promoters at a position immediately upstream of one or two FNs (Knight et al., 2014). Following depletion of Rap1 we carried out MNase-seq whole-genome analysis using two different MNase concentrations designed to capture even subtle effects at most FNs. Analysis of these data revealed significant changes in either nucleosome fragility, position, or occupancy at 300 regions, all of which were within 600 bp of a peak of Rap1 binding, suggestive of an immediate and localized effect of Rap1 depletion (Figure S5B). Overall, we found that a total of 133 FNs within 350 bp of a Rap1 peak displayed a >2-fold increase in stability as measured by occupancy ratio following Rap1 depletion. Notably, we found that in the cases where Rap1 depletion

significantly reduced the MNase sensitivity of a nearby FN, the affected particle generated fragments of increased length (Figure 5B). The effects of Rap1 depletion were complex, however, perhaps in part because Rap1 often binds nearby to two different, adjacent FNs. Overall, we identified four distinct scenarios associated with Rap1 depletion. In a few cases where weak Rap1 binding was detected within a NFR, the NFR narrowed upon Rap1 depletion (Figure 5C, leftmost panels). We also noted many cases in which Rap1 bound immediately upstream or within a FN that became stabilized upon Rap1 depletion (Figure 5C, two panels labeled ‘‘1 FN stabilized’’). Finally, in cases where Rap1 bound near two FNs, we observed examples in which either one or both of these FNs was stabilized upon Rap1 depletion (Figure 5C, four panels on right). The average behavior for the four cases described above is depicted in the top two panels of Figure 5D, and in schematic form in the bottom panel. At FNs found close to Rap1 peaks where its depletion had no apparent effect, we often found an additional GRF bound nearby (e.g., Reb1; Figure S5C). Indeed, we noted a significant average Reb1 binding signal at nucleosomes near Rap1 peaks that did not respond to Rap1 depletion, which was not the case at those FNs where Rap1 depletion led to changes (Figure S5D). In many cases (216 genes) Rap1 depletion led to a significant shift of the +1 nucleosome (Figure S5E). In 49 of these cases, the +1 shift coincided with upstream FN changes, while there were 91 examples where the shift occurred in the absence of any clear change at the upstream FN. A further 76 SN-type promoters displayed upstream shifts of the +1 nucleosome. Depletion of Abf1 or Reb1 also led to profound effects localized to sites where these proteins bind. In contrast to the case of Rap1, the size of region of chromatin affected by these GRFs was smaller, consistent with the observation that binding of these factors is on average centered on a single FN (Figure 5A) or within a NFR. We identified three different types of outcomes of these factor depletion experiments that were shared by both factors (Figure 6). First, in many of the cases where either Abf1 or Reb1 bound within a genuine NFR (average +1/1 distance 250–260 bp), its depletion resulted in a significant (50–60 bp) shrinkage of the NFR (Figures 6A and 6B; left panels). Second, for many genes where either factor had a peak of binding coincident with a FN (Kasinathan et al., 2014), depletion of the GRF rendered the FN undetectable. This was invariably associated with a shift of the adjacent SNs toward the site of GRF binding, such that the resulting NFRs, which were on average 165 bp when the GRF and the FN were present, were less than 90 bp following GRF depletion. We interpret the loss of the FN in these cases to be a direct consequence of NFR shrinkage, perhaps due to a failure to recruit RSC in the absence of the GRF. Indeed, many of these FNs were strongly affected by Sth1 depletion (Figure 3D). We note that, for the case of Reb1 these FNs were on average relatively low-occupancy even before Reb1 depletion, suggesting that Reb1, itself can effectively compete for binding with nucleosomes. Third, we observed a significant number of cases, for both factors, where depletion led to a clear stabilization (loss of MNase sensitivity) of the FN that was coincident with GRF binding. In these cases, the flanking SN

[Page 10]

binding was also significantly lower at promoters bound by both GRFs, which was not the case for Reb1 (Figure S6B). These results suggest that Reb1 is more stably bound and has a stronger influence on the shape of surrounding chromatin in comparison to Abf1. It has been suggested that Abf1 and Reb1 might participate in recruitment of RSC to promoters (Hartley and Madhani, 2009). To address this issue, we measured the overlap between genes whose NFRs or 1 FNs were affected by Abf1 or Reb1 depletion and the genes whose +1 nucleosome position was affected by depletion of Sth1 (Figure S6C). We found that the overlaps were relatively large, indicating that for many promoters both a GRF and RSC participate in establishing the promoter architecture. However, in most cases (except for Reb1-affected 1 FNs) the overlaps were not statistically significant (hypergeometric p value > 0.05), thus preventing us from concluding that the GRFs and RSC always act together. center-to-center distances were higher on average than for the cases where FN loss was observed, and GRF depletion did not reduce this distance. In addition, both Abf1 and Reb1 bound at a significant number of FNs and NFRs. At these loci it seemed that Reb1 has a dominant role since its depletion resulted in larger changes comparing to Abf1 depletion (Figure S6A). Abf1

Reassessing the Nucleosome-Free Region Two important features emerge from our analysis of promoter MNase digestion kinetics. First, genuine nucleosome-free promoter regions (i.e., stretches of DNA showing no evidence of protection even in limited MNase digests) are invariably less than 150 bp in length. Thus, although most genes display a nucleosome-free ‘‘spacer’’ region upstream of the +1 nucleosome of some length, this region rarely, if ever, exceeds the length of DNA required to form a canonical nucleosome. Weiner et al. (2010), who appear to have detected about half of the FNs that we describe here in a lower-resolution analysis, also noted that MNase-sensitive particles appear only where the flanking SNs leave an intervening stretch of DNA >150 bp. Our data therefore indicate that nucleosomes are densely packed genome wide and that the nuclear concentration of histone octamers, combined with their DNA affinity, is such that they will form nucleosomal particles (either stable or fragile) wherever a ‘‘free’’ region of DNA of sufficient length (150 bp) exists. Second, at over 2,000 promoters one or two nucleosomes immediately upstream of the +1 nucleosome exist in a distinctive dynamic state that renders them sensitive to MNase digestion. These ‘‘fragile’’ nucleosomes are not simply canonical nucleosomes that are present less frequently (i.e., ‘‘depleted’’ or ‘‘low occupancy’’) but instead, based upon their altered sensitivity to MNase, are physically different. We currently do not know whether the dynamic nature of FNs is due to more frequent unwrapping of DNA from the histone octamer core, rapid translational motion of the DNA over the histone core, direct destabilization by bound GRFs and/or chromatin remodelers, a suboctamer core composition, or some combination of these factors, but this is a question of considerable interest. We note that the promoter classification described here (1 SN versus 1 FN) is significantly different from previous categorizations of nucleosome architecture genome-wide ((Tirosh and Barkai, 2008; Zaugg and Luscombe, 2012) (see Figure S7; Tables S3, S4, and S5). Importantly, the classification reported here correlates more strongly with several different measures of transcriptional output, as well as expression noise, indicating

[Page 11]

Figure 7. Schematic Representation of Elements Determining Promoter Nucleosome Architecture Illustrations at the top represent hypothetical situations in the absence of both GRF binding and RSC remodeling, as inferred from anchor-away experiments. Small NFR formation (left column) is critically dependent on RSC working through the G/C-rich and polyA motifs (left). Large NFRs (second column from left) contain, in addition, one or more GRF binding sites and are formed through the concerted action of the GRF(s) and RSC. Their size never exceeds 150 bp, and the average +1 to 1 SN distance is 270 bp. At FN-containing promoters (middle right column), we identify two basal conditions, one in which the GRF binding site is present in linker DNA (left) and one in which it is embedded in a SN when the GRF is not present (right). These promoters possess more abundant and broadly distributed G/C-rich and polyA motifs than do NFRs. GRF binding at these promoters, in the absence of RSC, increases the stable +1 to 1 distance and in some cases displaces the SN near the GRF binding site (FN column, middle). The additional action of RSC at these promoters increases the +1 to 1 SN distance, permitting the formation of a 1 FN (red) that is destabilized by GRF action (FN column, bottom). Promoters containing two FNs (right-most column) are often bound by Rap1 and contain a large destabilized nucleosome region that forms through an unknown mechanism, potentially involving additional TFs and/or chromatin remodelers. In all four columns the right-most nucleosome in every illustration represents the +1 nucleosome, and the dashed gray and black arrows represent the TSS. The order of events presented in these schemes is hypothetical. We imagine that the actual in vivo situation is highly dynamic and may involve configurations of nucleosomes, GRFs, and remodelers not indicated here. See also Figure S7 for NFR size distributions and Tables S3, S4, and S5 for further details.

that it captures features of promoter nucleosome architecture, particularly with respect to position and stability, that are more relevant to transcription and its regulation. RSC, GRFs, and the Mechanisms Underlying Promoter Nucleosome Architectures Our analysis of sequence motifs that distinguish fragile from stable 1 nucleosomes, together with their relationship to RSC and GRF action, provides direct insight into mechanisms that mold promoter nucleosome architecture (Figure 7). FN-containing promoters are characterized by a broad distribution of two specific motifs (polyA and G/C-rich), both of which are concentrated at the NFRs of genes lacking FNs. Sth1 depletion experiments suggest that RSC acts through these promoter motifs to repel nucleosomes away from them, a conclusion directly supported by motif mutagenesis or insertion at individual promoters, and consistent with prior studies implicating RSC in nucleosome depletion at NFRs (Badis et al., 2008; Ganguli et al., 2014; Hartley and Madhani, 2009). Thus, at promoters with a 1 SN, RSC action increases the size of the NFR. At all other promoters, where the motifs are more widely distributed, we suggest that RSC helps to generate a larger open region that is populated by one or two FNs. We identify a number of promoters where NFR widening by RSC action appears to be necessary to permit FN formation, but only 11 cases genome-wide where RSC removal either significantly decreases the MNase-sensitivity of a FN or increases its occupancy. We note, though, that an MNase titration following RSC depletion will be required to rule out more subtle effects of RSC on the FN state genome-wide. Nevertheless, despite the fact that ATP-driven nucleosome remodeling and chaperonedriven nucleosome exchange are both highly active processes at promoter regions, our results (Figures 3 and S3; data not shown) suggest that they play a more subsidiary role in destabilizing the majority of FNs. Instead, we speculate that remodeling and histone exchange may instead be promoted by nucleosome fragility. Finally, we note that in the cases where RSC removal results in the disappearance of an FN, we cannot exclude the possibility that

RSC directly promotes FN formation at that site. In this scenario, the inward movement of flanking nucleosomes following RSC depletion might instead be a direct consequence of FN loss. Data presented here clearly show that RSC action alone is insufficient to determine promoter nucleosome location, since GRF depletion causes nucleosome displacement toward either the center of an NFR or FN region that is invariably equal to or greater than that observed following Sth1 removal, consistent with studies that measured promoter nucleosome occupancy at low resolution following Abf1 or Reb1 depletion (Hartley and Madhani, 2009). Indeed, our data suggest that GRFs may be broadly required to promote RSC activity, perhaps by helping to expose the polyA and G/C-rich motifs through which RSC acts, which might otherwise be blocked by nucleosome binding. Alternatively, or in addition, GRFs may physically interact with RSC to recruit it to promoters (Figure 7). We cannot rule out the possibility that RSC acts redundantly with other remodelers (Morris et al., 2014; Musladin et al., 2014), which might explain our finding that a sub-set of promoters are relatively insensitive to Sth1 depletion. Furthermore, our findings do not rule out the possibility that additional mechanisms, perhaps more directly linked to transcription initiation, influence promoter architecture, for example, by helping to fix the in vivo position of the +1 nucleosome (Rhee and Pugh, 2012; Struhl and Segal, 2013). Although our data clearly implicate GRFs in the destabilization of FNs, the underlying mechanisms are still unknown. Furthermore, it seems likely that multiple mechanisms are involved, since Rap1 can destabilize nucleosomes at quite some distance from where it is stably bound (>200 bp in many cases). For the case of Abf1 or Reb1, both of which typically bind close to the dyad axis of a FN, fragility might be generated by a competitive binding mechanism that takes advantage of transient DNA unwinding from the histone octamer core, as demonstrated in studies by Widom and colleagues (Li et al., 2005; Polach and Widom, 1995). Alternatively, Abf1 and Reb1 might sometimes promote the formation of a nucleosome on sequences where histone octamer binding is inherently unstable, explaining the many cases
[Page 12]

where their removal leads to disappearance of an FN. Finally, we speculate that Rap1 may sometimes act through auxiliary factors to destabilize nearby nucleosomes, though none of the candidate factors at ribosomal proteins genes that we have tested (Fhl1, Ifh1, or Hmo1) are individually required (data not shown). Understanding precisely what FNs are (e.g., histone content, modification state, structure, and DNA interactions), and how they are destabilized, are significant challenges for future studies. FNs and Transcriptional Regulation Our data suggest that histone octamer concentrations are such that promoter nucleosomes will be densely and relatively stably packed in vivo in the absence of active mechanisms to destabilize them. We argue that GRFs, in collaboration with the RSC chromatin remodeler, have evolved for precisely this function. We note that so-called ‘‘pioneer’’ TFs are likely to carry out similar functions at promoters and enhancers in more complex eukaryotes, perhaps in collaboration with multiple, partially redundant remodelers (Morris et al., 2014). This competitive balance between nucleosomes and GRFs may be the result of an evolutionary trade-off that helps to restrict the access of RNAPII to specific regions (promoters) in the genome. Consistent with this view, defects in nucleosome re-assembly in the wake of transcription (Kaplan et al., 2003), or deficiencies in histone expression and nucleosome formation in aging cells, can lead to inappropriate transcription (Hu et al., 2014). Because the FN promoter architecture is strongly associated with high levels of transcription, we speculate that this relationship is causal, and reflective of greater access to the promoter region. It is tempting to speculate that FNs may also participate in regulation. Since many of the genes containing FNs are controlled in a continuous manner by nutrient availability (Keren et al., 2013), despite having different TF configurations, modulation of promoter nucleosome stability may be a strategy used to coordinate growth-related transcription on a genome-wide level. It will thus be interesting to test in the future whether FNs participate directly in regulation and, if so, through precisely which mechanisms. EXPERIMENTAL PROCEDURES Yeast Strains Yeast strains used in this study are listed in Table S2. Micrococcal Nuclease Chromatin Digestion Chromatin for MNase digestion was prepared essentially as described (Kent and Mellor, 1995) (Supplemental Experimental Procedures). Anchor-Away and ChIP-Seq Experiments Anchor-away experiments were performed as described ((Haruki et al., 2008); Supplemental Experimental Procedures). For histone H3 and H2A ChIP-seq the cells were prepared as for MNase analysis up to the EDTA addition step, at which point the lysate was frozen. After thawing the samples were centrifuged for 15 min at 7,000 rpm. Anti-H3 or anti-H2A antibodies (Abcam) were then added to the supernatant. Following steps in the ChIP protocol were performed as described (Ribaud et al., 2012).

reverse transcriptase (Roche), and resulting cDNA was diluted 5- to 20-fold and quantified using qPCR. High-Throughput Sequencing, Mapping, and Nucleosome Occupancy Tracks DNA from MNase-digested chromatin was prepared for deep sequencing according to Henikoff et al. (2011) with minor modifications described in Supplemental Experimental Procedures. Mapping of paired-end reads, as well as data from publicly available databases, was done using HTSStation. Further details are given in Supplemental Experimental Procedures. Nucleosome Center and FN Determination In order to determine nucleosome dyad axis (center) positions from the MNase-seq data, we designed and implemented an algorithm (described in detail in Supplemental Experimental Procedures) that was able to locate signal peaks. For FN determination, we first identified nucleosome centers with minimal occupancy of eight reads/10 M and minimal center-to-center distance of 100 bp in the sample obtained using the lowest MNase concentration. We then derived an occupancy ratio by comparison to the data from high MNase samples. See Supplemental Experimental Procedures for details of this analysis. Details of all other computational analysis are given in Supplemental Experimental Procedures. ACCESSION NUMBERS All deep sequencing datasets have been submitted to the NCBI GEO under the accession code GEO: GSE73337. SUPPLEMENTAL INFORMATION Supplemental Information includes seven figures, seven tables, and Supplemental Experimental Procedures and can be found with this article online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.molcel.2015.10.002.
